Hi, I’m Robbie. I’m an adaptable software engineering leader, architect, and manager with experience working on different verticals across markets. Opinionated, driven and enjoyable to work with. Agile practitioner with all the trimmings. I’ve hired, built and run teams of over 100 people (not resources).

**Find me on LinkedIn:** [https://linkedin.com/in/robbieclutton](https://linkedin.com/in/robbieclutton)   
**Book an intro session:** [https://calendly.com/robbie-clutton/coaching](https://calendly.com/robbie-clutton/coaching)  
**Read more from Robbie:** [https://blog.robbieclutton.com](https://blog.robbieclutton.com) 

# How I can help

| Fractional CTO / VP | Projects  | Advising & Coaching |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| These engagements are typically ½ \- 3 days a week over a number of months. This might be in support of an organisation in **transition** or **transformation**.   Transition might be adding a second product line, growing headcount and in need of additional management and leadership, or taking on, or spinning up a special project.  Transformation may be building a software delivery competence for the first time, or moving towards an agile, lean or XP structure. | One off full day engagements from a day to multiple weeks. Engagements might be a review/audit/due-diligence of the idea-to-production or path-to-production value streams; training workshops or other custom agreements. Expectation of prep before, and review after of any engagement.  Intended for anyone in the organisation depending on the need. | Weekly 1:1s for 45-60 minutes  Expectation of 1-4 hours prep in support of the working session. Intended for the Senior Leadership Team in support through a transition or transformation or for Staff/Principal individuals and Senior Managers through to the executive leaders. |

# Where I can help

| Minimum viable products | People growth | Ways of working | Product-Led Growth | App Modernisation |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| I’ve supported clients in building their first MVPs of a new product \- either for a startup or new initiative for an enterprise. | I’ve grown teams from 15 to 50, led teams up to 120 people, opened new office locations and teams as well as had to handle downturns and closing locations. I’ve worked on recruiting processes, new hire onboarding, pay and promotion processes and more. | I’ve helped clients adopt and change their ways of working, incorporating lean product management, user centred design, extreme programming practices and continuous delivery processes.  | At Snyk I lead the engineering team in our PLG efforts, building teams focused on user acquisition, activation and self-service monetisation.  | I’ve worked with clients in iteratively modernising 20+ year old code bases moving to modern Cloud based deployments.  |

# Bio

Hello 👋. I’m one of those people who crosses the streams between technology and business leadership, and am looking to apply that experience through my independent business. I started down the management path in 2014 on what I would call an on-the-job MBA. I’ve grown teams from 15 to 50, run a European wide consultancy practice of over 120 people (made of engineers, designers, product managers, data scientists, programme managers and country leaders), promoted internally and recruited externally a diverse leadership team to run Pivotal Labs in the UK, Ireland, Germany, France and the Netherlands. At Pivotal, I have strived to hire great people, run great client engagements and create a highly productive and psychologically safe workplace. At Snyk I joined a hyper-growth scale-up, and helped them hire, launch self-serve and expansion capabilities, developer (customer) experiences, new products and supported key technical initiatives.

I first became a people manager in 2012 and moved from managing individuals, to managing managers, and then managers of managers across Pivotal, VMware and Snyk. I have strived to build environments where people can thrive, and would focus on systems to support that. My style is helping individuals through their day-to-day challenges with a view to their future goals. Offering collaborative stretch projects, feedback and growth for individuals, first time managers and those striving for senior leadership roles. 

# Q\&A with Robbie

**You have always been technical but not in a head down sense. And you have clearly shifted into management without difficulty. Has that been to look for wider horizons, more leadership or just following a natural upward trajectory?**

Even from BT days I’d had feedback that I was able to “talk with the business”, and through that I got some great opportunities like being invited to participate in acquisition due diligence and getting involved in programmes like the Microsoft Imagine Cup Innovation Accelerator.

I actually resisted going into the management path for a long time. It wasn’t until joining Pivotal Labs that I took my first step. There managers were still coding on projects 90-95% of the time. I felt I could dip my toe into management without feeling like starting a new career. Pivotal Labs was a consultancy (it has since been acquired by VMware and is now known as VMware Tanzu Labs) and as such as client facing. I was asked to join pre-sales meetings where we’d scope projects before writing a proposal. I enjoyed talking with clients, learning about their challenges and goals, and facilitating workshops. That evolved into taking on a role as part of the leadership group for Pivotal Labs in New York, where I was based for a few years. I felt going into that role that I could always go back to engineering if this didn’t work out, but I’m still on that path 9 years later. 

I grew up with Pivotal in many ways. The company grew from a thousand to three thousand and expanded across Europe, and that aligned with my growth into new roles: from supporting an office (first New York then London), to running London, before finally running our EMEA business. 

**What stages and sizes of team have you led in the past?**

I spent 9 years with Pivotal Labs (now VMware Tanzu Labs) where I started my management journey. This included managing IC engineers, managing managers and then managing director level.

* I managed the UK business from 15 to 50 people  
* I managed the EMEA business at a peak of 120 people (engineering, product, design, data science, program managers and country leaders) across the UK, Ireland, German, BeNeLux and France

Pivotal was spun out of EMC in 2013 after Pivotal Labs was acquired by EMC in 2012\. It was founded with about 1,000 people and grew to 3,500 people. Pivotal took Series A-C rounds before going public on the New York Stock Exchange in 2018 although it was mostly funded by VMware and Dell EMC \- companies which it had been spun out from. I was invited to the opening day. Pivotal was then acquired by VMware (\~35,000 staff) at the end of 2019\. 

![][image1]

At Snyk it was a more classic engineering and architects org. I joined managing 15 and ended up with about 60-70 engineers, architects, managers and directors.

Snyk was \~600 people when I joined (May ‘21) and \~1,500 people when I left (Nov ‘22). While I was with Snyk it went through Series E through G rounds. 

I’ve managed teams which created MVPs, modernised 20+ year old applications and much in between; ran engineering for a Product Led Growth (PLG) group at Snyk.

**What's been your biggest win from a business perspective?**

* Saved several people months of work by asking a series of questions regarding a required version change of a dependency, leading to the discovery of backwards compatibility mode which meant that re-work wasn’t needed.   
* Supported the team building the first version of Sainsbury’s [Chop Chop](https://chopchopapp.co.uk/), which saw it beat competing services on a Channel 4 TV show.   
* High retention and promotion from within at PIvotal into managerial and leadership roles.

**Are you happy to dig into technical stuff, devops, architecture, QA etc?**

Absolutely, although I haven’t written code in anger for several years and I’m pretty rusty there. Big picture and process is fine, but details I might not be the right fit. 

# Meet the CTO Craft Coach 

**Bio** 

\*\*High res picture to be provided by Coach if we haven’t already got one or you want to use a different one\*\*

[https://photos.app.goo.gl/gUVnAZgxpiibm97p9](https://photos.app.goo.gl/gUVnAZgxpiibm97p9) 

**Name:** Robbie Clutton

**Former positions:** VP Engineering, Snyk; Senior Director, VMware; Senior Director, Pivotal Labs

**Short bio (work, background, interests):** Hi, I’m Robbie. I’m an adaptable software engineering leader, architect, and manager with experience working on different verticals across markets. Opinionated, driven and enjoyable to work with. Agile practitioner with all the trimmings. I’ve hired, built and run teams of over 100 people (not resources).

**Interview Questions**

1. **Tell us about your life before coaching \- what roles and projects did you work on?**

I completed my degree in Software Engineering in 2005 and work at BT making phones ring, and the Guardian build content systems and great experiences before moving to New York City with Pivotal Labs support everything from one person startups to the global Fortune 50, building new applications or modernising 20+ year old applications and everything in between. I started as an engineer and moved up through first line manager, Associate Director for our NY practice before moving home to the UK, taking over the UK practice and onto our EMEA consultancy practice. I moved to Snyk in 2021 supporting Product-Led Growth (PLG), developer tools like IDEs and CICD tooling, and an education product, Snyk Learn. 

2. **Why did you choose to go into coaching?**

I wanted to bring what I’d learnt and developed in my manager experience of individuals, managers and directors and share that wider. I have also been inspired by *Trillion Dollar Coach* about Bill Campbell, and Michael Bungay-Stanier’s books on coaching.

3. **Do you have a coaching specialism or interest?**

I’m currently working towards my [Red Team Coaching](https://www.coaching.com/red-team-coaching/roof/) certification which offers a set of tools and processes to continuously improve management systems, and business plans to thrive in a volatile, uncertain, complex, ambiguous and hyperconnected environment. 

I would also frame myself more like a sports-coach, and mentor type of coach.

4. **How do you build trust between you and the person you’re coaching?**

We have two ears and one mouth. I try to use them in that ratio.

5. **Have you been coached before, and if so, what did you learn from it?**

I have. I learnt that in my effort to be helpful and efficient I was actually harming my teams’ own individual growth, and coming across as a bit of a jerk. It taught me that “perception is reality”, and that I needed to change the way I communicated, and tried to lead in order to build the team I hoped to build.

6. **How does your background in tech help you to coach individuals?**

I’m one of those people who crosses the streams between technology and business leadership. I can help non-technologists understand technology and technical colleagues, as well as help technologists understand the people and business side of the business.

7. **Can you tell us something interesting about you that not many people know?**

I was a ballboy at Arsenal for the 1997/98 season, and attended every game. I got to meet the players at an end of season celebration. I still have the shirt I asked the players to sign. Unfortunately I didn’t get to meet Ian Wright as he’d already moved to West Ham by the time the event happened.

8. **What are the key skills you think every coach needs?**

Staying curious \- with a nod to Michael Bungay-Stainer. 

9. **Can you tell us about when you really made a difference to someone as their coach and why?**

…

10. **At what stage in a tech leader’s career do you think they should have a coach?**

I don’t think there’s a bad time, but natural times would include transitions such as into a manager, into a manager or manager and into business unit leader. 

11. **If someone isn’t sure whether they would benefit from a coach, what would be your advice?**

I think that depends on the individual's starting place. From my experience, and what I’m seeing \- leadership roles can be lonely, you may be the only person in that role in a company. If you could have a confidant, someone who could help you overcome challenges, to help you grow as an individual and to challenge you to improve yourself, and your plans \- why would say no? 

12. **In your opinion, could even the most experienced tech leaders benefit from a coach and why?**

With a [nod](https://www.fastcompany.com/90331367/bill-campbell-silicon-valley-trillion-dollar-coach-book) back to *Trillion Dollar Coach*, Bill Campbell coached Larry Page, Sergey Brin, Eric Schmidt, Jonathan Rosenberg and Sundar Pichai at Google, Steve Jobs at Apple, Brad Smith at Intuit, John Donahoe at eBay, Marissa Mayer at Yahoo, Dick Costolo at Twitter, and Sheryl Sandberg at Facebook. With the right mindset, and the right coach, every person can benefit.

13. **What’s your favourite coaching exercise, podcast or recommended reading?**

For exercises, I’m a fan of Dr Gary Klein’s premortem analysis and a number of tools covered in Red Team Thinking (via book, podcast or training). 

For books, I’ve already mentioned Trillion Dollar Coach by Eric Schmidt, Jonathan Rosenberg and Alan Eagle; Coaching Habit by Michael Bungay-Stainer and Advice Trap by Michael Bungay-Stainer. All good reads. 

For podcasts, I often listen to [The Thinking Leader (podcast)](https://www.redteamthinking.com/podcasts/the-thinking-leader) and [Work Life with Adam Grant (podcast)](https://www.ted.com/podcasts/worklife).

[image1]: <data:image/png;base64,>
